commands.review_summary:
The PR removes memory-core's listener-level archive-file early return so reset/deleted transcript archives can be scheduled for live session reindexing, and adds a focused regression test for that path.

PR surface: Source -3, Tests +153. Total +150 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main returns before scheduling archive session files, while the later delta batch explicitly handles usage-counted reset/deleted archives. I did not run tests in this read-only review, but the code path and contributor before/after terminal proof are coherent.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] No repair job is needed; the PR has no blocking findings, and the remaining action is maintainer merge judgment or optional broader end-to-end proof.

target.title: fix(memory-core): keep live reindex of reset/deleted session archives
target.body:
## Summary
After a `/reset` or a session delete, the archived conversation is missing from memory search until the next gateway restart. The reset/deleted transcript is renamed to `<id>.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `<id>.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` and an `emitSessionTranscriptUpdate({ sessionFile })` is fired, but the memory-core session listener now drops that update before scheduling, so the archive is never indexed on the live path.

This is a regression from #89912 and reintroduces #57334 (memory-core indexer skipping `.jsonl.reset`/`.jsonl.deleted`, past sessions invisible to search). The incremental indexing of those archives was added in #76520, and the emit comment in `src/gateway/session-transcript-files.fs.ts` documents that this event is the sole live channel because chokidar does not watch the sessions directory.

## Root cause
`extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts`, inside `ensureSessionListener`. #89912 added a guard that returns early for any archive artifact before it can be scheduled:

```ts
// before
const sessionFile = update.sessionFile;
if (sessionFile && isSessionArchiveArtifactName(path.basename(sessionFile))) {
  return;
}
if (sessionFile && this.isSessionFileForAgent(sessionFile)) {
  this.scheduleSessionDirty(sessionFile);
  return;
}
```

`.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` live under the agent's sessions directory, so before the guard they passed `isSessionFileForAgent` and reached `scheduleSessionDirty(sessionFile)`. `processSessionDeltaBatch` has a dedicated branch that marks usage-counted archives (`isSessionArchiveArtifactName && isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName`) dirty directly and triggers a sync. The new guard makes that branch unreachable from the live listener, leaving only the gateway-restart startup catch-up to ever pick the archive up.

## Fix
```ts
// after
const sessionFile = update.sessionFile;
if (sessionFile && this.isSessionFileForAgent(sessionFile)) {
  this.scheduleSessionDirty(sessionFile);
  return;
}
```

In-agent archives fall through to `scheduleSessionDirty` exactly as before #89912, so the usage-counted-archive branch in `processSessionDeltaBatch` indexes them live again. `resolveSessionTranscriptUpdateSyncTarget` already returns `null` for archive paths, so the `target` path below is unaffected and there is no double scheduling.

## Why this is the right boundary
The listener is the single live entry point for session-file mutations into memory sync, and the downstream archive handling it feeds (`processSessionDeltaBatch`) already exists and is unchanged. Restoring the fall-through is the minimal canonical fix; no second path is introduced. The guard's only effect was to drop work the next stage was built to handle. The sibling startup path (`runSessionStartupCatchup`) already lists usage-counted archives, so both the live and restart paths now agree. Open PR #76666 only changes when the listener is attached at gateway startup and does not touch this guard; it is complementary, not overlapping.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` on the new test plus the adjacent `manager-sync-ops.archive-delta-bypass.test.ts` and `manager-sync-ops.startup-catchup.test.ts`: 17 passed. New test fails on pristine main (archive never scheduled) and passes with the patch.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs`, `oxfmt --check`, and `tsgo` on `tsconfig.core.json` and `tsconfig.core.test.json`: clean.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: a `/reset` or session-delete archive emit is dropped by the memory listener, so the reset/deleted conversation is not indexed into memory search until the next gateway restart.
Real environment tested: drove the real `MemoryManagerSyncOps.ensureSessionListener` and `processSessionDeltaBatch` through the real 5 second dirty-debounce timer, on pristine main `bc243568` and on the patched tree, with an identical on-disk `thread.jsonl.reset.<iso>` archive and an identical emitted update; the archive file, the listener guard, and the delta-bypass scheduling stayed real, and only the final embedding-provider index write was replaced with a no-op.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: write `<sessionsDir>/thread.jsonl.reset.2026-06-23T10-00-00.000Z`, attach the real listener, emit `{ sessionFile }`, wait out the 5s debounce, then read back the scheduler state.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main bc243568)
emitted session transcript update for: thread.jsonl.reset.2026-06-23T10-00-00.000Z
scheduled for live indexing after emit: false
marked dirty after debounce window: false
index sync scheduled: false
# AFTER (this patch, identical inputs)
emitted session transcript update for: thread.jsonl.reset.2026-06-23T10-00-00.000Z
scheduled for live indexing after emit: true
marked dirty after debounce window: true
index sync scheduled: true
```
Observed result after fix: on pristine main the reset archive emit scheduled nothing and never reached the index, while with the patch the same emit scheduled the archive, marked it dirty, and queued the index sync.
What was not tested: no live embedding-provider request was issued, the index write itself was not exercised end to end, and a full gateway build was not run.
